skill.name: GraphQL Architect
skill.description: Master modern GraphQL with federation, performance optimization, and enterprise security. Build scalable schemas, implement advanced caching, and design real-time systems.
skill.summary: Expert GraphQL architect for enterprise-scale schema design, federation, and performance optimization.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 lines and detected no suspicious patterns. This is a prompt-only skill containing instructional content for GraphQL architecture guidance. No executable code, network access, filesystem operations, or external command execution present.

content.user_title: Design scalable GraphQL APIs with enterprise patterns
content.value_statement: Building production GraphQL systems requires expertise in federation, performance, and security. This skill provides proven patterns for scalable schema design and optimization.
actual_capabilities: Design federated GraphQL schemas with Apollo Federation v2 and subgraph patterns
Implement DataLoader caching and query optimization to eliminate N+1 problems
Configure field-level authorization, rate limiting, and introspection security
Build real-time subscription systems with WebSocket and server-sent events
Create migration strategies from REST APIs to GraphQL with backward compatibility
Generate type-safe clients using GraphQL Code Generator and schema tooling
limitations: Cannot execute code or deploy GraphQL servers directly
Requires manual implementation of suggested schemas and resolvers
Does not replace dedicated GraphQL testing and monitoring tools
Recommendations assume standard GraphQL server environments

use_cases.title: Enterprise Federation Architecture
use_cases.description: Design a multi-team federated GraphQL architecture with subgraph composition, schema governance, and cross-team collaboration patterns.
use_cases.target_user: Backend architects leading GraphQL adoption across multiple engineering teams

use_cases.title: Performance Optimization Audit
use_cases.description: Analyze existing GraphQL schemas for N+1 queries, implement DataLoader patterns, and configure multi-level caching strategies.
use_cases.target_user: Performance engineers optimizing high-traffic GraphQL APIs

use_cases.title: Security Hardening Implementation
use_cases.description: Implement field-level authorization, query complexity analysis, rate limiting, and production security configurations.
use_cases.target_user: Security engineers responsible for GraphQL API protection

prompt_templates.title: Basic Schema Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GraphQL schema for a [resource type] with CRUD operations. Include appropriate scalar types, input types for mutations, and follow naming conventions. Explain the type relationships.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a new GraphQL API from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Federation Subgraph Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an Apollo Federation v2 subgraph for [domain]. Identify entities, key directives, and external type references. Show how this subgraph integrates with the overall federated architecture.
prompt_templates.scenario: Distributing GraphQL architecture across multiple teams

prompt_templates.title: Performance Optimization Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this GraphQL schema and resolver implementation for performance issues. Identify N+1 query patterns, suggest DataLoader implementations, and recommend caching strategies at field and query levels.
prompt_templates.scenario: Optimizing an existing GraphQL API for production traffic

prompt_templates.title: Security Configuration Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this GraphQL server configuration for security vulnerabilities. Check introspection settings, CORS configuration, rate limiting, query depth limits, and field-level authorization. Provide specific remediation steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: Hardening a GraphQL API before production deployment

output_examples.input: Design a user profile schema with posts and comments

output: Use DataLoader to batch and cache user post fetching:
This reduces N queries to 1 batched query.

best_practices: Design schemas with versioning and evolution in mind - use deprecation before removal
Implement DataLoader for all resolver fetching to prevent N+1 query problems
Configure query complexity analysis and rate limiting before production deployment
anti_patterns: Exposing database models directly in GraphQL schema without abstraction layers
Returning unbounded lists without connection patterns or pagination
Implementing business logic in resolvers instead of dedicated service layers

faq.question: What is Apollo Federation and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Apollo Federation enables splitting a GraphQL schema across multiple services. Use it when multiple teams own different domains or when microservices need to expose unified GraphQL APIs.

faq.question: How do I prevent N+1 query problems in GraphQL?
faq.answer: Use DataLoader to batch and cache database requests. DataLoader collects individual requests and executes them as a single batch query, reducing database round trips significantly.

faq.question: What security measures are essential for production GraphQL APIs?
faq.answer: Implement query depth limiting, query complexity analysis, rate limiting, field-level authorization, disable introspection in production, and configure proper CORS headers.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication in GraphQL resolvers?
faq.answer: Extract authentication from context in your Apollo Server setup. Access user information via context.user in resolvers and implement field-level checks using middleware or directive patterns.

faq.question: What is the difference between queries and mutations?
faq.answer: Queries fetch data and can be cached. Mutations modify data and always execute. Use queries for read operations and mutations for create, update, or delete operations.

faq.question: How do I implement real-time features in GraphQL?
faq.answer: Use GraphQL subscriptions with WebSocket connections. Define subscription types in your schema, implement pubsub mechanisms, and ensure proper authorization and cleanup on disconnect.
